summary: DeVault surveys programming languages he's familiar with, offering blunt assessments of each language's strengths and weaknesses for different use cases. He advocates for knowing enough about many languages to make informed decisions about which tool fits a given problem. His preferred languages include C, Go, Python, and POSIX shell, while he reserves particular contempt for C++, PHP, and Ruby. The post ends with deliberately provocative dismissals of languages he considers irredeemable, making no attempt at diplomatic balance.
key_points: The best approach to language selection is learning enough about many languages to reason about their fitness for a problem, even without deep expertise in all of them
C and Go receive the most favorable treatment as practical, well-specified tools with clear strengths in systems and server programming
Rust is acknowledged as useful but criticized for being too large and having only one meaningful implementation
C++, PHP, and Ruby are dismissed entirely with no listed pros, reflecting DeVault's uncompromising style
A recurring concern across languages is ecosystem health, specification quality, and whether multiple implementations exist
money_quotes: The best way is to learn by doing, so getting a general impression of the utility of many languages helps equip you with the knowledge of whether or not they'd be useful for a particular problem even if you don't know them yet.
C has footguns, some programmers in the wild think the footguns are useful.
Rust is far too big, non-standardized, only one meaningful implementation.
JavaScript's package ecosystem is a flaming pile.
Haskell is written by people who wish the world could be described with a pure function and design software as if it could.
C++ pros: none. Cons: ill-defined, far too big, loads of baggage, enjoyed by bad programmers.
Every PHP programmer is bad at programming, the language is designed to accommodate them with convenient footguns (or faceguns) at every step.
Ruby is both ENTERPRISE and HIP at the same time, and therefore effective at herding a group of junior to mid-level programmers in a certain direction, namely towards your startup's exit.
key_insight: Pragmatic language selection requires broad familiarity with many tools and honest assessment of their tradeoffs, not loyalty to any single ecosystem.
